does: This paper introduces a machine unlearning method designed specifically for multilingual language models that can selectively erase memorized information across multiple languages while preserving model performance. The core contribution is an adaptive unlearning scheme that assigns language-dependent weights to account for varying performance levels across different languages in multilingual models. The method addresses a critical vulnerability where unlearning sensitive information in a high-resource language does not guarantee removal from low-resource languages, leaving models exposed to extraction attacks through less dominant languages. The empirical results demonstrate that the proposed framework effectively removes targeted knowledge across languages while maintaining overall model capability, outperforming existing monolingual unlearning baselines when applied to multilingual settings.
connects: This work builds on the growing body of research in machine unlearning, which emerged as a practical alternative to full model retraining when removing private or copyrighted data. Previous unlearning methods focused exclusively on monolingual models, employing techniques like gradient ascent and knowledge distillation to selectively forget information. The paper connects to broader safety concerns in large language models regarding memorization and privacy, as highlighted by studies on training data extraction and membership inference attacks. It also draws on multilingual model research, recognizing that these architectures exhibit language imbalances and differential memorization patterns. The work responds to the gap in unlearning literature by extending monolingual techniques to the cross-lingual setting and identifying language-specific vulnerabilities that naive application of existing methods fails to address.
enables: This research opens multiple directions for safer multilingual AI systems. It enables development of privacy-preserving multilingual models where sensitive information can be reliably removed regardless of language, addressing regulatory requirements like GDPR across different linguistic communities. The adaptive weighting scheme could extend to other multilingual challenges involving performance imbalances across languages. Future work could investigate whether similar cross-lingual vulnerabilities exist in other unlearning scenarios or model types, potentially motivating new defenses. The framework provides a foundation for studying language-dependent memorization patterns more broadly, which could inform better training practices for multilingual models. Additionally, this work points toward practical deployment scenarios where models need certification of information removal across diverse languages, supporting trust and compliance in global AI applications.
